## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	
8	## Current Concept
9	
10	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
11	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
12	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module). Neither the umbrella nor the
13	implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in BUS0. Architectural spine
14	in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
15	
16	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
17	
18	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
19	  rarely. Identifier: viceroyalty.
20	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Four fields:
21	  `BURP_NODE` (viceroyalty reference), `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`,
22	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`, `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from
23	  privkeys via `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline
24	  strings. Identifier: investiture.
25	
26	**BURC addition:**
27	
28	- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
29	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
30	
31	**Garrison-destructive model:**
32	
33	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
34	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
35	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
36	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
37	
38	**Cross-cutting premises:**
39	
40	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
41	  it by reference, never duplicates.
42	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
43	  material; operator owns all key administration.
44	
45	**Tabtarget colophons (13 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
46	
47	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
48	- `buw-jpg[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
49	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
50	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
51	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
52	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
53	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1
54	
55	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
56	
57	BCG-compliant module hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive
58	mappings (b/c/w), the workload privkey destination path on remote per
59	shell-letter, and the canonical WSL distribution name (`rbtww-main`). SSH
60	transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries no shell selection.
61	
62	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter):**
63	
64	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` using `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` (key auth
65	   only — no password path; first-time bootstrap is operator-manual via
66	   handbook before garrison runs)
67	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from privkey via `ssh-keygen -y`)
68	   in admin's authorized_keys with the shell-letter's `command=` directive
69	3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
70	   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
71	   are workload's own concern, not garrison's
72	4. Create fresh workload user named `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` (unprivileged: no
73	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access)
74	5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with `command=`
75	6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
76	   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
77	   outbound to GitHub)
78	7. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0
79	
80	## Heat Sequence
81	
82	**Spec foundation:**
83	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
84	
85	**Implementation:**
86	- implement-jurisdiction — drop BURW infrastructure, four-field BURP,
87	  `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`, `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 13 tabtargets,
88	  garrison ceremony, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries
89	
90	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
91	- AAe — localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
92	
93	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
94	- AAD — WSL distro + Cygwin install
95	- AAE — JJK fundus inside WSL
96	- AAF — Docker dual-daemon
97	
98	## Deferred (named for future return)
99	
100	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpgp` tabtarget + the `p`
101	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
102	  only.
103	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
104	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
105	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
106	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
107	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
108	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
109	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
110	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
111	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
112	
113	## Standing Notes
114	
115	- **Garrison assumes admin SSH key trust is pre-established.** First-time
116	  bootstrap (initial pubkey placement, password-auth disable on the node) is
117	  operator-manual via the handbook. Garrison uses key auth only; on key
118	  failure it errors out with a pointer to the bootstrap procedure.
119	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
120	  prompts interactively; garrison would hang. Operator policy: privkeys
121	  unencrypted; station-level security covers privkey-at-rest protection.
122	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
123	  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
124	  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
125	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
126	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
127	  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
128	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
129	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
130	  path construction.
131	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
132	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
133	- Fundus capability registry: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSFR-FundusRegistry.md`.
134	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
135	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
136	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
137	  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
138	
139	# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAl
140	
141	## Character
142	Spec rewrite — declarative, mostly mechanical given the paddock as ground truth.
143	
144	## Docket
145	Rewrite BUS0 §Remote Node Access to reflect the jurisdiction shape declared in
146	the heat paddock. Paddock is the source; spec restates as needed.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module). Neither the umbrella nor the
implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in BUS0. Architectural spine
in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: viceroyalty.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Four fields:
  `BURP_NODE` (viceroyalty reference), `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`,
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`, `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from
  privkeys via `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline
  strings. Identifier: investiture.

**BURC addition:**

- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (13 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpg[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive
mappings (b/c/w), the workload privkey destination path on remote per
shell-letter, and the canonical WSL distribution name (`rbtww-main`). SSH
transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries no shell selection.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter):**

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` using `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` (key auth
   only — no password path; first-time bootstrap is operator-manual via
   handbook before garrison runs)
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from privkey via `ssh-keygen -y`)
   in admin's authorized_keys with the shell-letter's `command=` directive
3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
   are workload's own concern, not garrison's
4. Create fresh workload user named `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access)
5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with `command=`
6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
   outbound to GitHub)
7. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — drop BURW infrastructure, four-field BURP,
  `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`, `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 13 tabtargets,
  garrison ceremony, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- AAe — localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- AAD — WSL distro + Cygwin install
- AAE — JJK fundus inside WSL
- AAF — Docker dual-daemon

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpgp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.

## Standing Notes

- **Garrison assumes admin SSH key trust is pre-established.** First-time
  bootstrap (initial pubkey placement, password-auth disable on the node) is
  operator-manual via the handbook. Garrison uses key auth only; on key
  failure it errors out with a pointer to the bootstrap procedure.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; garrison would hang. Operator policy: privkeys
  unencrypted; station-level security covers privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
  path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- Fundus capability registry: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSFR-FundusRegistry.md`.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

content.text:
## Mounted ₣A- — rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures (12/20 racing)

**Paddock summary:** Windows test infrastructure for RB. **Jurisdiction** = BUK feature-area for reaching remote nodes; logical umbrella, untyped in AXLA. Two regimes: **BURN** (node-shape, viceroyalty) and **BURP** (privileged+workload creds, four-field, investiture). New `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` global. Garrison-destructive model — no conscript/discharge/withdraw/inventory/vanquish; one workload user per node, ever. 13 tabtargets across regime/garrison/dispatch/handbook (PowerShell deferred). Operational seat: `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` hardcodes shell-letter mappings + WSL distro `rbtww-main`.

**Recent paces:** Two paddock-curry commits (4ca15e1b, 1db66a72) refined the shape — review-pass clarifications + jurisdiction-shape redirect.

**Next pace:** ₢A-AAl `spec-bus0-jurisdiction` [rough] — BUS0 rewrite; declarative/mechanical given paddock as ground truth.

**Pace name:** "spec-bus0-jurisdiction" fits — file + concept. No rename.

**Approach (proposed):**
1. Read current BUS0 to inventory what's there now (mapping section, §Remote Node Access narrative, BURP/BURW quoins, tabtarget catalog, subdoc includes).
2. Edit BUS0 — mint `bus_jurisdiction` + `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` quoins; rewrite BURP to four-field shape; rip BURW section + `burw_lieutenancy` + `burn_workloads`; replace tabtarget catalog with paddock's 13.
3. Retire 11 subdocs (8 garrison/conscript/withdraw/inventory + 3 BUSN platform docs); mint 7 new jurisdiction subdocs.
4. Grep sweep for dangling `<<undefined-anchor>>` and dropped vocabulary (lieutenancy, conscript, withdraw, vanquish, inventory, multi-resident, per-platform garrison).

**Execution strategy:** Sequential opus — single spec file with concept-model/MCM discipline, plus subdoc create/delete. Mechanical in shape but requires judgment on quoin minting (sub-letter discipline) and grep-cleanup completeness. Not parallelizable — all edits funnel through BUS0 and its include graph.

Proceed?

### diagnose-localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation (₢A-AAe) [rough]

## Character
Investigation. Reproduction is known and reliable; root cause not classified.

## Docket
JJK localhost fundus scenario tests fail 6/8 in parallel and pass 8/8 sequentially.

Reproduce on curia at this commit or later (fundus at same commit):

- Fails 6/8: `cargo test --manifest-path Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/Cargo.toml --test jjtlg_fundus_scenario localhost::`
- Passes 8/8: append `-- --test-threads=1`

Failure pins in `zjjtlg_preflight_happy` (`Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/tests/jjtlg_fundus_scenario.rs`). The preflight SSH's `jjfu_full@localhost` and runs BUK tabtarget `buw-rcr.RenderConfigRegime.sh`; under concurrent SSH load this returns exit 1, while the same command run manually one-at-a-time returns exit 0. The contended path is BUK dispatch (writable state in logs / last-pointer / temp), not JJK — `buw-rcr` is BUK-side and unrelated to the BURH→BURN rename in AAW.

Pre-existing vs regression is not classified. First mount step: A/B against parent commit `44014598` (the BUF_EXT_ALIAS fix, before AAW). If parallel still fails 6/8 there, it's a pre-existing BUK parallelism bug; if it passes, bisect inside AAV/AAW. Then either fix the BUK contention or document `--test-threads=1` as mandatory for the JJK fundus scenario in JJSTF.

### bootstrap-windows-admin-trust (₢A-AAo) [rough]

## Character
Operator-driven review + handbook rewrite — assess prior drafts, salvage what's
sound, rewrite the rest. Quality of prior tries is uncertain.

## Docket
Garrison assumes admin SSH key trust is already established. On a fresh Windows
host, that trust must be created out-of-band before garrison can run. Land a
clean operator-facing procedure for that bootstrap.

Existing draft material to review (entry points, not exhaustive):
- `tt/buw-HW{bs,sc,vs}` tabtargets (BootstrapSshd, SshConfig, VerifySsh)
- `Tools/buk/buhw_windows.sh` related functions
- `BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc` (if surviving AAl)

Done when:
- Handbook section reachable from `buw-hj0` walks the operator through
  first-time Windows admin SSH bootstrap end-to-end: install sshd (per chosen
  shell variant), place initial admin pubkey, disable PasswordAuthentication,
  verify key-only auth works from operator's station
- Procedure terminates at a node where `buw-jpg{b,c,w}` garrison succeeds on
  the first invocation
- Prior draft content either rewritten cleanly under the new design or marked
  for AAn cleanup; AAn's docket updated if the disposition differs from its
  current "retire" assumption
- Operator-tested on a real Windows host (not review-then-commit only)

unrelated legacy detritus (AAn).

### diagnose-localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation (₢A-AAe) [rough]

## Character
Investigation. Reproduction is known and reliable; root cause not classified.

## Docket
JJK localhost fundus scenario tests fail 6/8 in parallel and pass 8/8 sequentially.

Reproduce on curia at this commit or later (fundus at same commit):

- Fails 6/8: `cargo test --manifest-path Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/Cargo.toml --test jjtlg_fundus_scenario localhost::`
- Passes 8/8: append `-- --test-threads=1`

Failure pins in `zjjtlg_preflight_happy` (`Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/tests/jjtlg_fundus_scenario.rs`). The preflight SSH's `jjfu_full@localhost` and runs BUK tabtarget `buw-rcr.RenderConfigRegime.sh`; under concurrent SSH load this returns exit 1, while the same command run manually one-at-a-time returns exit 0. The contended path is BUK dispatch (writable state in logs / last-pointer / temp), not JJK — `buw-rcr` is BUK-side and unrelated to the BURH→BURN rename in AAW.

Pre-existing vs regression is not classified. First mount step: A/B against parent commit `44014598` (the BUF_EXT_ALIAS fix, before AAW). If parallel still fails 6/8 there, it's a pre-existing BUK parallelism bug; if it passes, bisect inside AAV/AAW. Then either fix the BUK contention or document `--test-threads=1` as mandatory for the JJK fundus scenario in JJSTF.

lastPrompt: CAn we do this pace in parallel with the next one?

content.text:
Looking at AAl + AAo together — they have soft dependencies, not hard:

**Hard ordering signals:**
1. AAo's docket explicitly says "**BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc** (if surviving AAl)" — disposition decided by AAl (paddock retires it).
2. AAo's deliverable is "handbook section **reachable from `buw-hj0`**" — but `buw-hj0` is one of the 7 new jurisdiction subdocs AAl mints. AAo's content lives under AAl's structure.

**What's actually independent:**
- AAl: BUS0 spec edits + subdoc retire/mint + grep sweep — pure file work.
- AAo's review phase (read `buw-HW{bs,sc,vs}`, `buhw_windows.sh`, judge salvage) — pure read.
- AAo's operator-on-Windows-host testing — human-driven, agent doesn't do it anyway.
- AAo's handbook write — could draft against the paddock directly (paddock is the source of truth, not BUS0).

**Risk if run in parallel:**
- Both touch the BUSNW question (AAl deletes; AAo reviews). Order doesn't matter if AAl wins on the delete.
- `buw-hj0` subdoc: AAl mints the skeleton; AAo writes substantive bootstrap content into it. Race risk if both try to author the same file.
- AAo's sub-tabtargets under `buw-HW*` — if any survive cleanup (deferred to AAn), they may need to align with AAl's new `buw-jpg{b,c,w}` naming.

**My recommendation:** Sequential. AAl is fast-mechanical (single-file cluster, paddock as ground truth), and running it first means AAo can write bootstrap handbook content directly into the post-AAl structure without merge friction. Forcing parallel buys little — the AAo phase that benefits most (operator testing on Windows) is human-side anyway and not blocked by AAl.

If you want to parallelize regardless, the clean split is: **agent does AAl now**; you (operator) start AAo's review-of-prior-drafts + Windows-host bootstrap testing in parallel, then I pick up AAo's handbook authorship after AAl lands. Want that?

257	== Overview
258	
259	The Bash Utilities Specification defines the tabtarget dispatch system — the infrastructure for tab-completion-friendly launchers that route to kit formularies.
260	
261	BUK invented tabtargets; BUK defines them.
262	
263	For universal naming vocabulary (Cipher, Signet, Epithet, Inscription, Vesture), see VLS-VoxLiturgicalSpec.adoc.
264	
265	== Tabtarget Vesture
266	
267	[[bust_tabtarget]]
268	//axl_voices axo_entity
269	{bust_tabtarget}::
270	Vesture for tabtarget launcher identifiers.
271	Defines how launcher scripts in `tt/` are named.
272	+
273	* signet_case: lowercase with hyphen (the colophon)
274	* separator: `.`
275	* epithet_case: PascalCase (the frontispiece)
276	* envelope: `.sh`
277	+
278	Pattern: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
279	+
280	Examples:
281	+
282	* `rbw-cr.Rack.sh` — RBW workbench, Rack operation
283	* `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — same, with imprint specifying target
284	* `jjw-.HeatMount.sh` — JJK workbench, HeatMount operation
285	* `buw-tt.LauncherList.sh` — BUK tabtarget testbench, LauncherList operation
286	
287	== Dispatch
288	
289	The dispatch vocabulary defines the tabtarget launcher system.
290	A
291	{busdl_launcher}
292	validates arguments and delegates to a
293	{busdm_formulary}.
294	The
295	{busdc_colophon}
296	identifies the routing path;
297	the
298	{busdf_frontispiece}
299	provides human-readable description;
300	the
301	{busdi_imprint}
302	embeds target parameters.
303	
304	[[busdc_colophon]]
305	//axl_voices axt_string
306	{busdc_colophon}::
307	The routing identifier in a tabtarget inscription.
308	A
309	{busdc_colophon}
310	determines which
311	{busdm_formulary}
312	handles the dispatch.
313	Includes the hyphen as part of its identity (e.g., `rbw-`, `buw-tt-`).
314	+
315	The
316	{busdc_colophon}
317	is the first component of the tabtarget pattern, before the first dot separator.
318	+
319	Examples:
320	+
321	* `rbw-` routes to RBW workbench
322	* `jjw-` routes to JJK workbench
323	* `buw-tt-` routes to BUK tabtarget testbench
324	
325	[[busdf_frontispiece]]
326	//axl_voices axt_string
327	{busdf_frontispiece}::
328	The human-readable description in a tabtarget inscription.
329	A
330	{busdf_frontispiece}
331	uses PascalCase and describes the operation.
332	+
333	Appears after the
334	{busdc_colophon}
335	and dot separator.
336	+
337	Examples: `Build`, `Rack`, `RunVVX`, `HeatMount`.
338	
339	[[busdi_imprint]]
340	//axl_voices axt_string
341	{busdi_imprint}::
342	An optional embedded parameter in a tabtarget inscription.
343	An
344	{busdi_imprint}
345	specifies a target, instance, or configuration variant.
346	+
347	Appears after a second dot separator following the
348	{busdf_frontispiece}.
349	+
350	Examples:
351	+
352	* `tadmor` in `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
353	* `srjcl` in `rbw-cr.Rack.srjcl.sh`
354	
355	[[busdm_formulary]]
356	//axl_voices axo_entity
357	{busdm_formulary}::
358	A component that routes
359	{busdc_colophon_s}
360	to implementations.
361	Each
362	{busdm_formulary}
363	registers handlers for a set of
364	{busdc_colophon_s}
365	and dispatches to the appropriate function.
366	+
367	Types:
368	+
369	* **Workbench** (`{prefix}w_workbench.sh`): Routes commands for a kit
370	* **Testbench** (`{prefix}t_testbench.sh`): Routes tests for a kit
371	+
372	A
373	{busdm_formulary}
374	is sourced by the
375	{busdl_launcher}
376	and provides the dispatch function.
377	
378	[[busdl_launcher]]
379	//axl_voices axo_entity
380	{busdl_launcher}::
381	A bootstrap script in the `tt/` directory that validates arguments and delegates to a
382	{busdm_formulary}.
383	A
384	{busdl_launcher}
385	is the entry point for tabtarget invocation.
386	+
387	Naming follows
388	{bust_tabtarget}:
389	`{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
390	+
391	Responsibilities:
392	+
393	* Parse
394	{busdc_colophon}
395	from own filename
396	* Validate required arguments
397	* Source the target
398	{busdm_formulary}
399	* Invoke the dispatched function
400

= Bash Utilities Specification
:doctype: book

// tag::mapping-section[]
//
// Category declarations:
// bust_:  Tabtarget (launcher naming vesture)
// busd:   Dispatch (non-terminal, tabtarget system umbrella)
// busdc_: Dispatch Colophon (routing identifier)
// busdf_: Dispatch Frontispiece (human-readable description)
// busdi_: Dispatch Imprint (embedded parameter/target specifier)
// busdm_: Dispatch Formulary (routing component)
// busdl_: Dispatch Launcher (bootstrap script)
// bukro_: Kit Regime Operation (kindle/validate/render)
// burc_:  Configuration Regime (project-level settings)
// burn_:  Node Regime (per-node SSH connection profiles, multi-instance)
// burs_:  Station Regime (developer-level settings)
// burd_:  Dispatch Runtime (ephemeral state assembled during dispatch)
// bure_:  Environment Regime (ambient caller-supplied behavioral overrides)
// busi:   Invocation (test invocation umbrella)
// busn_:  Node Access (verbs for remote node management)
// busit_: Invocation Tier (tier classification)
// busig_: Invocation Global (capture state set by zbuto_invoke)
// busx_:  Case Boundary (test case isolation runner)
// busff_: Fact-File (inter-step communication via output files)
// buf_:   Fact Module (dual-write fact-file primitives)
// busy_:  Yelp (pre-rendered formatted string capture)
// burx_:  Exchange Regime (cross-project dispatch state)
//
// Variant patterns:
//   _s: plural form
//   _p: possessive form
//
